title: TypeScript Ladder Game
instructions: Fix one reviewed TypeScript example at a time. Each step gives you a plain-language goal, a hint, and a short reason before Lantern unlocks the next example.
scenario: This demo stays inside Lantern's governed browser-grader boundary. Learners do not run arbitrary TypeScript here. Instead, they repair ten reviewed examples that move from beginner type fixes to narrowing, generics, and exhaustive checks.
reviewed_note: Each checkpoint compares your edited snippet against one reviewed fix, so the feedback stays focused on the current checkpoint instead of running arbitrary TypeScript.
completion_note: Solve all ten examples, then finalize the ladder so Lantern can score the full reviewed set.

steps.title: Fix the broken primitive type
steps.prompt: This first snippet will not typecheck. The value is already correct. Fix the mistaken primitive annotation before Lantern unlocks step 2.
steps.goal: Make the type annotation match the numeric value.
steps.hint: Only one token is wrong on the first line.
steps.why_it_matters: A type annotation should describe the value you already have. The value 3 is a number, so the annotation should say number too.
steps.success_note: The variable now advertises the correct primitive type.

steps.title: Add function types
steps.prompt: This formatter works in JavaScript, but the reviewed TypeScript version needs explicit parameter and return types.
steps.goal: Add types for both parameters and for the returned lesson label.
steps.hint: Add two parameter types and one return type.
steps.why_it_matters: Function signatures tell TypeScript what goes in and what comes back, which helps catch mistakes before runtime.
steps.success_note: The function now explains its inputs and output clearly.

steps.title: Repair the array annotation
steps.prompt: The reducer adds numeric values, so the array should advertise numeric items too.
steps.goal: Tell TypeScript this array holds numbers, not strings.
steps.hint: The values inside the array are already numbers.
steps.why_it_matters: Array annotations describe every item inside the array. This reducer expects numeric values all the way through.
steps.success_note: The array type now matches the numeric values it stores.

steps.title: Correct an object type alias
steps.prompt: This card shape is close, but one property is typed incorrectly for the value that follows.
steps.goal: Change the minutes property so the type matches the numeric value below.
steps.hint: The object value is fine. Fix the property type declaration.
steps.why_it_matters: Object type aliases should match the data shape they describe, property by property.
steps.success_note: The card type now matches the object data.

steps.title: Narrow a union safely
steps.prompt: This function handles both strings and numbers, but it calls a string-only method before checking which branch it received.
steps.goal: Check whether the value is a string before using a string-only method.
steps.hint: Use typeof before you call toUpperCase().
steps.why_it_matters: Union types require narrowing first, so TypeScript knows which methods are safe in each branch.
steps.success_note: The function now handles both strings and numbers safely.

steps.title: Make a property optional
steps.prompt: This lesson can exist without a due date. Update the type so the object stays valid without inventing fallback data.
steps.goal: Mark dueDate as optional so the object can omit it.
steps.hint: Use the optional-property marker on dueDate.
steps.why_it_matters: Optional properties let you describe data that may be present without inventing placeholder values.
steps.success_note: The type now matches an object that may or may not include a due date.

steps.title: Turn a helper into a generic
steps.prompt: The helper returns the first item from any array. Replace the blunt unknown types with a generic that keeps the array item type intact.
steps.goal: Use a generic so the function returns the same item type that went into the array.
steps.hint: Introduce one generic type parameter and use it in both places.
steps.why_it_matters: Generics preserve useful type information instead of falling back to unknown everywhere.
steps.success_note: The helper now keeps the array item type all the way through.

steps.title: Fix the async return type
steps.prompt: Async functions always return promises. Update the signature so the reviewed declaration matches runtime behavior.
steps.goal: Update the signature so the async function returns a promise.
steps.hint: Wrap the string return type in Promise<...>.
steps.why_it_matters: Async functions always wrap their result in Promise, even when the inner value is a string.
steps.success_note: The return type now matches async behavior.

steps.title: Render a discriminated union
steps.prompt: This union exposes different safe properties in each branch. Use the kind field to read the correct property and return the reviewed label.
steps.goal: Use kind to choose the safe property for each branch.
steps.hint: score belongs to pass. reason belongs to fail.
steps.why_it_matters: Discriminated unions let TypeScript narrow to the right shape when you check the shared label field.
steps.success_note: Each branch now reads only the property that actually exists there.

steps.title: Finish with an exhaustive switch
steps.prompt: The final example is missing one state and one safeguard. Add the remaining branch, then end the switch with a never check so future changes fail clearly.
steps.goal: Add the missing done branch and finish with a never safeguard.
steps.hint: Add the done case first, then use a never fallback inside default.
steps.why_it_matters: Exhaustive switches make future changes obvious because TypeScript flags any state you forgot to handle.
steps.success_note: The switch now covers every state and fails clearly if a new one appears.
